content.value: A data structure is a way of organizing data in memory so it can be accessed and modified efficiently. The array is the simplest one: a fixed-size, contiguous block of memory holding elements of the same type, accessed by index in constant time.

int arr[5] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};
printf("%d", arr[2]); // 30, O(1) access via base address + index*size

Because array memory is contiguous, inserting an element at position i requires shifting every element from i onward one slot to the right, then placing the new value. This makes insertion O(n) in the worst case.
Deleting an element at position i requires shifting all later elements one slot to the left to close the gap - also O(n).

Array access is O(1) - fast and predictable, which is why arrays are preferred when you mostly read data. But insertion/deletion in the middle is O(n), which is why other structures like linked lists exist for data that changes frequently.
Always track the current logical size of the array separately from its allocated capacity - a classic source of off-by-one bugs in exams.

quiz.question: What is the time complexity of accessing arr[i] in an array?
Why is insertion into the middle of an array O(n)?
options: Because all elements must be re-shifted to make room
Because arrays are stored in linked nodes
